items.foco: Classification according to location (submucosal, intramural, subserous)
items.title: Classification by location
items.contentHtml:
Classification according to location

  • Submucosal myoma: originates under the endometrium and protrudes into the uterine cavity; It causes intermenstrual bleeding and is the most associated with infertility due to distortion of the endometrium.
  • Intramural myoma: located in the thickness of the myometrium; It is the most common type and usually manifests itself with menorrhagia and pelvic pain.
  • Subserous myoma: it grows towards the peritoneal serosa; It may be asymptomatic or cause compressive symptoms (frequency, constipation).
  • FIGO Classification (Fibroid Categorization System): submucosal type 0-2; intramural type 3-4; subserous type 5-7; transmural type 8.

items.foco: Most common symptom: heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia)
items.title: Heavy menstrual bleeding
items.contentHtml:
Heavy menstrual bleeding (menorrhagia)

  • Menorrhagia is defined as menstrual bleeding >80 ml per cycle or that interferes with quality of life; It is the most prevalent symptom in myomatosis, present in up to 50% of patients.
  • Pathophysiology: increase in endometrial surface area, vascular congestion and alteration of local coagulation due to factors such as vascular endothelial growth factor.
  • Chronic bleeding can cause iron deficiency anemia; Initial evaluation includes blood count and iron profile.
  • Differential diagnosis: endometrial polyp, adenomyosis, endometrial hyperplasia, coagulation disorders (von Willebrand disease).

items.foco: Association with infertility and recurrent pregnancy losses
items.title: Infertility and recurrent abortion
items.contentHtml:
Association with infertility and recurrent pregnancy losses

  • Submucosal fibroids are those that most affect fertility: they alter embryo implantation and increase the risk of spontaneous abortion in the first trimester.
  • Intramural fibroids >5 cm or those that distort the cavity are associated with reduced implantation rates in infertilization vitro.
  • Recurrent gestational losses are related to submucosal fibroids due to compression of the gestational sac and alteration of placental blood flow.
  • myomectomy improves pregnancy rates and reduces the risk of abortion in women with submucosal or intramural cavitary fibroids.

items.title: Transvaginal ultrasound
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis of choice: transvaginal ultrasound

  • Transvaginal ultrasound is the first-line test with >95% sensitivity to detect uterine fibroids; allows evaluation of number, size, location and echogenicity.
  • Typical findings: well-defined, hypoechoic mass, with a whorled appearance due to bundles of smooth muscle.
  • hysterosonography (saline infusion) improves the precision in classifying submucosal fibroids and evaluating the endometrial cavity.
  • MRI is reserved for complex cases (multiple fibroids, suspicion of leiomyosarcoma or surgical planning).

items.foco: First-line medical treatment: NSAIDs and hormonal contraceptives
items.title: Initial medical treatment
items.contentHtml:
First-line medical treatment: NSAIDs and hormonal contraceptives

  • NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600-800 mg every 8 hours during menstruation) reduce menstrual bleeding by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis and decreasing uterine blood flow.
  • Combined hormonal contraceptives (ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel) stabilize the endometrium and reduce blood loss; They are used cyclically or continuously.
  • The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device (IUD-LNG 52 mg) is highly effective in reducing menorrhagia, with bleeding reduction by up to 90% at 6 months.
  • Tranexamic acid (antifibrinolytic) 1 g every 6 hours for 5 days reduces menstrual loss by 40-50%.

items.foco: Myomectomy indicated in women with a desire for future fertility
items.title: Myomectomy and fertility
items.contentHtml:
Myomectomy indicated in women with a desire for future fertility

  • Myomectomy is the conservative surgical treatment of choice in patients with symptomatic fibroids and a desire for pregnancy; It is performed hysteroscopically (submucosal), laparoscopic or laparotomic.
  • Operative hysteroscopy is the standard for submucosal fibroids types 0 and 1; the recurrence rate is 10-20% at 5 years.
  • Risks: intrauterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome) after hysteroscopic myomectomy; Uterine dehiscence in subsequent pregnancy if the incision was transmural.
  • It is recommended to wait 3-6 months before attempting pregnancy to allow adequate myometrial healing.

items.foco: Hysterectomy is the definitive treatment in symptomatic cases
items.title: Definitive hysterectomy
items.contentHtml:
Hysterectomy as definitive treatment

  • Hysterectomy (total or subtotal) is the definitive curative treatment for symptomatic myomatosis when parity has been completed or there is no desire for future fertility.
  • Indications: heavy bleeding refractory to medical treatment, severe pelvic pain, significant compressive symptoms or chronic anemia.
  • The approach can be abdominal, laparoscopic or vaginal; Vaginal hysterectomy is associated with less morbidity and faster recovery.
  • Risks: ureteral or bladder injury, hemorrhage, infection, early menopause if bilateral oophorectomy is performed; ovarian preservation should be discussed in premenopausal patients.

items.foco: Red degeneration (aseptic necrosis) more common in pregnancy
items.title: Red degeneration in pregnancy
items.contentHtml:
Red degeneration (aseptic necrosis)

  • Red degeneration is an aseptic hemorrhagic necrosis that typically occurs in fibroids during pregnancy, especially in the second trimester, due to relative ischemia and increased blood flow.
  • Clinic: acute abdominal pain, mild fever and uterine tenderness; It can simulate an acute obstetric abdomen.
  • Diagnosis: ultrasound shows myoma with heterogeneous areas and hypoechoic halo; MRI reveals high signal intensity on T1 due to hemorrhage.
  • Management: conservative with rest, analgesics (paracetamol) and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs avoided in the third trimester due to the risk of premature closure of the ductus arteriosus.

items.foco: Leiomyosarcoma: differentiation due to rapid growth and cellular atypia
items.title: Differentiation with leiomyosarcoma
items.contentHtml:
Leiomyosarcoma: differentiation

  • Leiomyosarcoma is a malignant smooth muscle tumor that is differentiated from myoma by rapid growth (increase in size in a few months), cellular atypia, high mitoses (>10 mitoses per 10 high-power fields) and tumor necrosis.
  • Risk factors: age >40 years, history of radiotherapy pelvic, tamoxifen, and retinoblastoma inheritance syndrome.
  • Image: MRI shows irregular borders, heterogeneous signal on T2 and diffusion restriction; Ultrasonography may suggest malignancy due to disorganized central vascularization.
  • Definitive diagnosis requires histopathological study; preoperative biopsy has low sensitivity, and laparoscopic morcellation of a leiomyosarcoma worsens the prognosis.

items.foco: GnRH agonists (leuprolide) for presurgical size reduction
items.title: GnRH agonists
items.contentHtml:
GnRH agonists (leuprolide) for presurgical reduction

  • Leuprolide (gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist) is administered intramuscularly or subcutaneously every 1-3 months; induces a reversible hypoestrogenic state that reduces fibroid volume by 30-50% at 8-12 weeks.
  • Main indication: presurgical reduction in tumor size to facilitate myomectomy or hysterectomy, especially in large (>10 cm) or multiple fibroids.
  • Adverse effects: vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes), vaginal dryness, loss of bone density; should not be used for more than 6 months without low-dose hormone replacement therapy (add-back therapy).
  • Contraindications: pregnancy, lactation, untreated osteoporosis; Discontinuation of the drug causes reappearance of the original size in 3-6 months.

items.foco: Uterine artery embolization as a conservative alternative
items.title: Uterine artery embolization
items.contentHtml:
Uterine artery embolization

  • Uterine artery embolization is a minimally invasive procedure that selectively occludes the arteries that supply the myoma using particles (PVA or microspheres), causing ischemic necrosis and volume reduction by 40-60% at 3 months.
  • Indicated in symptomatic patients without desire for future fertility or as an alternative to hysterectomy; the improvement rate of menorrhagia is 85-90%.
  • Contraindications: pregnancy, active pelvic infection, uncorrected coagulopathy, suspected leiomyosarcoma, severe renal failure.
  • Complications: post-embolization syndrome (pain, fever, nausea) self-limited in 48-72 hours; risk of permanent amenorrhea if there is embolization of ovarian collaterals (5-10% in women <45 years).

items.foco: Submucosal fibroids associated with spontaneous abortion and intermenstrual bleeding
items.title: Submucosal fibroids and intermenstrual bleeding
items.contentHtml:
Submucosal fibroids: abortion and intermenstrual bleeding

  • Submucosal fibroids (FIGO types 0-2) distort the endometrial cavity and are associated with recurrent spontaneous abortion, intermenstrual bleeding and severe menorrhagia.
  • Intermenstrual bleeding is due to ulceration of the mucosa. overlying and vascular congestion; diagnostic hysteroscopy confirms the lesion.
  • The risk of abortion in the first trimester is 2-3 times greater in the presence of submucosal myomas, due to alteration of decidualization and placental blood flow.
  • Treatment: hysteroscopic myomectomy is the standard; improves pregnancy rates and reduces abnormal bleeding in >90% of cases.
